title: Surface coating induced lubrication in flowing granular materials
authors: Sayali V. Chaudhary, Ashish V. Orpe
abstract: We investigate the flow of spherical, bulk granular particles down an inclined plane mixed with small-sized spherical lubricant particles using discrete element method simulations. Predefined cohesive interaction is implemented between lubricant and bulk particles, enabling the coating of the former over the latter. The overall flow rate exhibits non-monotonic dependence on lubricant content. Initially, it increases with lubricant addition, reaches a maximum at an intermediate lubricant content, and decreases for even higher lubricant content. The increase in the flow rate is attributed to a lower inter-particle friction coefficient between lubricant-coated bulk particles. The decrease in the flow rate at higher lubricant content, on the other hand, is attributed to enhanced densification and increased damping between crowded particles. Both these occurrences are examined using various flow level characteristics. The simulation results are found to be in qualitative agreement with previous experimental results. Overall, the outcome integrates novel computational insights and prior experimental results to enhance the understanding of the powder lubrication phenomena.
summary: This paper investigates how adding small lubricant particles to a granular flow creates a surface coating that changes flow dynamics. Using DEM simulations, the authors show that while low amounts of lubricant reduce friction and speed up the flow, excessive amounts lead to crowding and slower flow. The study provides a microscopic look at how particle contacts and surface coverage drive these macroscopic changes, bridging the gap between previous experimental observations and computational insights.
main_problem: Understanding the particle-level dynamics and mechanisms behind how surface coating by small lubricant particles alters the flow properties of bulk granular materials.
main_result: The flow rate exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on lubricant content, increasing due to reduced inter-particle friction and decreasing at higher concentrations due to enhanced densification and damping.
method: Discrete Element Method (DEM) simulations using LIGGGHTS software, calibrated against previous experimental data.
model_or_system: A mixture of large spherical bulk particles and small spherical lubricant particles flowing down a 24-degree inclined plane, with cohesive interactions modeled via the SJKR framework.
key_observables: Overall flow rate, velocity profiles, packing fractions, granular temperature (RMS velocity), and surface coverage.
important_parameters: Number ratio of lubricant to bulk particles (Nr), particle size ratio, chute inclination, and cohesion energy density.
main_assumptions: Lubricant particles are approximated as spheres rather than plate-like shapes, and periodic boundary conditions are used which may prevent the study of wall-induced segregation.
figures_summary: Schematic of the simulation setup; comparison of simulation and experimental velocity profiles; side-view images showing layer expansion and contraction; plots of surface coverage, contact types, and coordination numbers vs. lubricant content.
paper_structure: Introduction of the lubrication problem, description of the DEM simulation setup and contact models, presentation of flow rate and layer thickness results, analysis of particle-level contact networks and surface coverage, and comparison between cohesive and non-cohesive regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic showing the inclined chute configuration and the bulk particles. (a) View from the side. (b) Perspective view exhibiting all three directions. The blue spheres represent bulk particles, and the gray spheres denote the rough bottom surface. See text for more details.

FIG. 2. Variation of steady-state, normalized, flow direction average velocity (vx/vx,max) profile with normalized distance from the chute bed (z/dp) in simulations and experiments (unpublished). vx.max represents the maximum velocity in the flowing layer at the free surface and dp denotes the bulk particle diameter. See text for more details.

FIG. 3. Instantaneous configurations (images) from simulations as viewed from the side at steady-state for different values of (Nr). Bulk particles are represented as large blue spheres and lubricant particles as small yellow spheres. Cohesive interaction between bulk and lubricant particles is as per eq. (3). Multimedia available online

FIG. 4. Variation of steady-state mean volume fraction (⟨ϕ⟩) of particles (bulk, lubricant, and mixture) in the flowing layer with the number ratio (Nr). Error bars are smaller than the symbol size and hence are not shown to ensure clarity in the figure. See text for more details.
FIG. 5. Variation of steady-state, normalized, mean flow rate (⟨Q⟩/d2 p p
FIG. 6. Variation of steady-state, normalized, flow direction average velocity (vx −vs)/ p
FIG. 7. (a) Variation of steady-state, normalized, averaged rms velocity ⟨u⟩/ p

FIG. 8. Variation of mean bulk particle surface area coverage (⟨σ⟩) with the number ratio (Nr). The error bars represent spatial variations at any time instant and temporal variations over all time instants.

FIG. 9. (a) Variation of average number of binary contacts with the number ratio (Nr). The y-axis on the left side denotes contacts between bulk particles (⟨nc(pp)⟩). The y-axis on the right side denotes contact between bulk and lubricant particles (⟨nc(pl)⟩) and between lubricant particles (⟨nc(ll)⟩). (b) Variation of average coordination number for bulk particles (⟨Zp⟩) and lubricant particles (⟨Zl⟩) with the number ratio (Nr). The arrows are provided on the curves for ease in understanding. Error bars are smaller than the symbol size and hence are not shown to ensure clarity in the figure.

FIG. 10. Instantaneous configurations (images) from simulations as viewed from the side at steady-state for different values of Nr. Bulk particles are represented as large blue spheres and lubricant particles as small yellow spheres. Cohesive interaction is absent between bulk and lubricant particles. Multimedia available online
